dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Well, I guess... I mean, there’s this wedding coming up. My cousin’s wedding. I got the invitation, but I just, I don’t know. It feels like a lot, you know? I didn’t even respond to it. I just... ignored it. I feel really anxious thinking about it, like I’m going to be bombarded with questions or something. And then there's my mom, and how she always seems to think less of me. It just makes me not want to go at all.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds like the wedding is bringing up a lot of anxiety for you, especially with the thought of facing questions and your mom’s judgments. Ignoring the invitation seems like a way to cope with those feelings. Can you share more about what specifically makes you anxious about attending?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: Yeah, I mean, it’s just... everything feels overwhelming when I think about it. Like, people will want to know what I’m doing with my life, and I don’t have good answers for that. And then there's my mom, who’ll probably make some comment that’ll just sting. It’s like I can already hear her saying something judgmental, and that just makes me want to hide away. I feel like I don’t fit in with my family anyway, like they don’t really want me there.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like the thought of attending the wedding brings up a lot of pressure for you, especially around the expectations from your family and your mom's potential comments. You mentioned feeling like you don’t fit in with them. Can you tell me more about what that feels like for you?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: It just feels like... like I'm always on the outside looking in, you know? Like everyone else is having these great lives and I’m just... here. I mean, I know I don’t have much to show for myself, and it feels like they all notice that. It’s just awkward, and I get this knot in my stomach. I start thinking, “Why would they even want me there?” So, I just avoid it altogether. It’s easier than facing that feeling of not belonging.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: Therapist: It sounds really painful to feel like you’re on the outside and that your accomplishments aren’t being recognized. That knot in your stomach seems to signal how much this affects you emotionally. When you think about avoiding the wedding, it feels like a way to protect yourself from those uncomfortable feelings of not belonging. What do you think keeps you feeling this way about your place in the family?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess it's just... I don’t know, it feels like a pattern or something. Like, I’ve always been the one who doesn’t fit in, you know? Growing up, I was always the bigger kid, and that made me feel different. And then my mom, she never really helped with that. It’s like she always had these expectations, and I could never meet them. So now, I just think they see me as this disappointment, and it’s hard to shake that feeling. I don’t know if they ever really liked me for me. It's like I have to suppress that part of myself to avoid being rejected again.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those early experiences of feeling different and not meeting your mom's expectations have deeply shaped how you view yourself within your family. That sense of needing to suppress parts of yourself to avoid rejection must be really heavy to carry. Can you share more about what it was like growing up with those feelings, and how they might connect to how you see your family now?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I mean, growing up was... complicated, I guess. It’s like I always felt this weight on my shoulders, like I was the odd one out. Kids can be really cruel, and I dealt with a lot of teasing because of my weight. And then my mom, she never really helped. I was just kind of left to figure it out on my own, which made everything worse. I guess I started to think that if I just faded into the background, maybe no one would notice how different I was. But then it just... stuck with me, you know? Now, when I think about family gatherings, I get this sense of dread. Like, why would they want me there? It's like I’m still that kid who doesn’t belong.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like those experiences of teasing and feeling unsupported by your mom really contributed to that sense of dread and the belief that you don’t belong. It must be exhausting to carry that feeling of being the "odd one out" into your adult life, especially in situations like family gatherings. When you think about fading into the background, it seems like a way to protect yourself from that pain. How do you feel about the idea of reconnecting with that part of yourself that wants to be seen and valued?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, it just feels really scary to think about being seen, you know? Like, what if they still don’t like me? Or what if I end up just feeling worse? I mean, it’s easier to hide away and avoid all that. I guess I’ve gotten used to it, just pretending that it doesn’t matter. But then, I sit around feeling lonely and sad, and it’s like this never-ending cycle. It's frustrating because I know I should want to connect with them, but... I just can't shake that fear of rejection.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re caught in a really tough cycle between wanting connection and fearing rejection. The idea of being seen feels overwhelming because of those past experiences, and yet there’s also a sense of frustration with the loneliness that comes from hiding away. It makes sense that you'd feel hesitant to reach out when you have those fears. What do you think would help you feel a little more comfortable in those moments of wanting to connect, even if it feels scary?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess... I don’t really know what would help, honestly. It just feels like too much to even think about connecting right now. Maybe if I had some kind of script or something to go off of, like, “This is what you say when they ask about your life,” then I wouldn’t feel so exposed. But I know that’s not real, right? It’s not like I can just pretend to be someone else, but the idea of being vulnerable feels daunting. I just keep circling back to wanting to avoid it altogether. I mean, every time I think about it, I can feel that weight in my chest again, and I just want to... I don't know, disappear or something.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re feeling really overwhelmed by the idea of vulnerability and connection, and having a script might feel like a way to protect yourself from that exposure. It’s understandable to want to avoid those heavy feelings, especially when they come with such a strong sense of pressure. The weight in your chest seems to reflect just how daunting this all feels for you. Can you tell me more about what it is about being vulnerable that feels so daunting?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess it just feels like... being vulnerable means opening myself up to all that judgment again, you know? Like, what if they see me and just think I’m a failure or something? It’s hard enough dealing with those feelings inside, and I don’t want to put myself out there just to get hurt again. It’s like I’ve built this wall around myself, and if I let someone in, what if they just knock it down and I’m left standing there, exposed? I don’t know... it’s just really daunting to think about. It feels safer to stay hidden.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like vulnerability feels like a risk for you, and the fear of judgment and being hurt again makes it hard to consider opening up. That wall you've built seems to offer a sense of safety, but I can hear how isolating it can also be. It’s understandable to want to protect yourself from those painful experiences. When you think about that wall and the safety it provides, what do you feel is on the other side that you might be missing out on?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don't know... I mean, I guess what's on the other side is just... connection? Like, I see people laughing and having fun together, and it makes me feel like I’m missing out on something important. But then, I think about all the times I’ve felt judged or dismissed, and it just makes me want to curl up and hide. It’s like there’s this part of me that craves that connection, but then there's the fear that it’ll just lead to more hurt. So, I don’t know... it’s really hard to even imagine what it would be like to be open and not feel that weight of judgment. It’s like I’m stuck in this loop.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you’re experiencing a real push and pull between wanting connection and fearing the judgment that can come with it. That craving for connection feels so strong, yet the memories of past hurt keep you feeling stuck in this loop of hiding. It must be really frustrating to feel torn like that. When you think about what connection could look like for you, even in small ways, what do you imagine it might feel like?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess if I think about connection, it might feel... I don’t know, comforting? Like, maybe I wouldn’t feel so alone if I could just share a laugh or a moment with someone. But then I start to spiral, you know? I think about how it could go wrong, like what if I say something dumb or they don’t respond the way I hope? It’s just confusing because I want that warmth and understanding, but the fear of getting hurt makes it hard to even picture. So, it’s like I’m stuck between wanting to feel that connection and being terrified of what could happen if I actually try. It's frustrating, really.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like you have a deep desire for that comforting connection, yet the fear of potential missteps and hurt creates a barrier that feels hard to breach. It’s understandable to feel frustrated being caught between those two sides—wanting warmth and understanding but feeling anxious about the risks involved. When you think about those small moments of connection you mentioned, what do you think might be a step you could take, even if it feels tiny, to move toward that warmth?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I guess... I don’t know. Maybe I could try just reaching out to one person, like a cousin or something, and see how that goes? But then I start thinking, what if they don’t want to talk or they don’t care? It feels like such a small step, but it still feels huge, you know? I mean, even just sending a message makes my heart race. I guess I just want to feel that warmth, but the fear always feels like it’s right there, waiting to pounce. It’s frustrating because I know I should try, but it’s like my brain is just stuck in this loop of what-ifs.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like reaching out to someone feels like a significant step for you, even though the fear of what-ifs is really present. That heart racing must be tough to manage when you’re trying to take a small action. It’s understandable to feel both the desire for warmth and the weight of those fears. What do you think might help you navigate that anxiety when you consider reaching out to your cousin?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know, honestly. I mean, maybe I could write out what I want to say first, so it doesn’t feel so overwhelming when I actually do it? But then again, what if they don’t respond or they don't care? It just spirals from there, and I end up feeling like it’s all pointless. I guess... maybe having a plan could help, but it still feels like such a big deal. I don’t know how to get past that anxiety. It’s just... frustrating, you know? It’s like I want to try, but part of me is just screaming to stay hidden.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like having a plan might provide a bit of structure and support when you're feeling anxious about reaching out. Yet, that fear of being ignored or dismissed can feel really discouraging, almost like it pulls you back into hiding. It’s completely understandable to feel frustrated by that push and pull. When you think about that anxiety of reaching out, what do you think is the most challenging part for you in those moments?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I think the most challenging part is just... the anticipation, you know? Like, I start imagining all the ways it could go wrong before I even try. My mind just races with thoughts like, “What if they don’t respond? Or what if they do and it’s awkward?” It feels heavy, like I’m carrying this weight of all those what-ifs around. And then I start to shut down, like it’s easier to just not do anything at all. I mean, I know I want to reach out, but it’s like there’s this part of me holding back, just waiting for something to go wrong. It’s exhausting.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like that anticipation of potential negative outcomes really weighs on you, creating a heaviness that makes it difficult to take action. It's understandable that you'd feel exhausted navigating all those what-ifs before even having a conversation. When those thoughts start to spiral, how do you usually cope with that overwhelming feeling?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I mean, when those thoughts start to spiral, I usually just... shut down, I guess. I try to distract myself with something else, like watching a show or scrolling through my phone, just to avoid dealing with it. But then I end up feeling even more alone and sad, like I'm just running away from everything instead of facing it. I know I should probably find a better way to cope, but it’s hard to break that cycle when it feels so comfortable to just... hide, you know? It’s frustrating because I want to feel like I can handle it, but it’s like I just keep getting pulled back into that dark place.

dialogue.speaker: Therapist
dialogue.text: It sounds like distracting yourself is a way to cope when those overwhelming thoughts arise, even though it often leaves you feeling more lonely and sad afterward. That desire to hide feels really strong, and I can see how it creates a cycle that’s tough to break. It’s understandable to lean into what feels comfortable, even if it doesn’t ultimately help you feel better. When you think about breaking that cycle, what do you think would make it easier for you to face those feelings instead of retreating?

dialogue.speaker: Client
dialogue.text: I don’t know... I mean, part of me thinks maybe if I could just take a moment to breathe and really sit with those feelings instead of running from them, it might help. But then again, it feels so uncomfortable, like I’m just standing on the edge of a cliff and afraid to jump. I guess I could try to remind myself that it’s okay to feel anxious or uneasy, but it’s hard to shake that urge to just retreat. It’s frustrating because I know hiding isn’t the answer, but facing it feels like this giant mountain I have to climb. I don’t know if I’m ready for that.
